From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Animal messengers in Shinto

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f4/Shinshi_001.jpg/250px-Shinshi_001.jpg)](/wiki/File:Shinshi_001.jpg)

Chickens at [Ise Grand Shrine](/wiki/Ise_Grand_Shrine "Ise Grand Shrine"). Parishioners believe they are messengers of [Amaterasu](/wiki/Amaterasu "Amaterasu").[[1]](#cite_note-D.-2011-1)[[2]](#cite_note-Ise_Shrine-2013-2)[[3]](#cite_note-Olderr-2017-3)[[4]](#cite_note-Goto-2015-4)

***Shinshi*** (神使; lit. '[spirit](/wiki/Kami "Kami") envoy')[[5]](#cite_note-Digital_Museum-5) are [animals](/wiki/Animal "Animal") in [Japanese mythology](/wiki/Japanese_mythology "Japanese mythology") that are believed to be associated with a *[kami](/wiki/Kami "Kami")*, a divine being. These animals are also known as **kami* no tsukai* or *tsukawashime*. In ancient texts such as *[Kojiki](/wiki/Kojiki "Kojiki")* and *[Nihongi](/wiki/Nihon_Shoki "Nihon Shoki")*, there are tales of special animals that acted on behalf of the *kami* to transmit the divine will or to bear oracles.[[5]](#cite_note-Digital_Museum-5)

Over time animals were connected to certain shrines. It became a custom to take care of these animals when they were found within the area of the shrine. Normally, each *kami* had only one animal familiar, but sometimes, there were some exceptions where a *kami* had more than one. Even some of the "[Seven Lucky Gods](/wiki/Seven_Lucky_Gods "Seven Lucky Gods")" like [Daikokuten](/wiki/Daikokuten "Daikokuten") (a [mouse](/wiki/Mouse "Mouse")) and [Benzaiten](/wiki/Benzaiten "Benzaiten") (a [snake](/wiki/Snake "Snake")) had animal familiars.[[5]](#cite_note-Digital_Museum-5)

Later the *kami's* animal familiar became a common symbol of the *kami* itself. For example, the foxes at Inari shrines was worshipped as a manifestation of [Inari Ōkami](/wiki/Inari_%C5%8Ckami "Inari Ōkami"). These creatures were thought to be extraordinary spiritual beings, and this perception, combined with their relationship with the specific *kami*, likely gave rise to this phenomenon.[[5]](#cite_note-Digital_Museum-5)

It probably originated in [shamanic](/wiki/Shamanic "Shamanic") practices, where animals aided shamans in traveling to the spirit world. Different deities have different associated animals, such as foxes for [Inari Okami](/wiki/Inari_Okami "Inari Okami") and [deer](/wiki/Deer "Deer") with [Kasuga](/wiki/Kasuga-taisha "Kasuga-taisha").[[1]](#cite_note-D.-2011-1)

Many tribal communities viewed their shaman's [familiar](/wiki/Familiar "Familiar") as an ancestor, and this may have influenced the connection between animals and spirits in Shinto.[[1]](#cite_note-D.-2011-1) For example, the [Kamo clan](/wiki/Kamo_clan "Kamo clan") believed that [Yatagarasu](/wiki/Yatagarasu "Yatagarasu") was their ancestor [Kamotaketsunumi no Mikoto](/wiki/Kamotaketsunumi_no_Mikoto "Kamotaketsunumi no Mikoto").[[1]](#cite_note-D.-2011-1)[[6]](#cite_note-6)

At [Ise Jingu](/wiki/Ise_Grand_Shrine "Ise Grand Shrine"), roosters roam around and are believed to be the assistants of the sun goddess, [Amaterasu](/wiki/Amaterasu "Amaterasu"). They wake her up every morning, according to folklore. Some experts believe that the rooster may be the bird depicted on the *[torii](/wiki/Torii "Torii")*, a gate that marks the entrance to a shrine.[[1]](#cite_note-D.-2011-1) They are believed to call up the dawn with their sounds.[[4]](#cite_note-Goto-2015-4)

[Inari Okami](/wiki/Inari_%C5%8Ckami "Inari Ōkami")'s fox messengers are considered to be her,[[7]](#cite_note-7) although both [Shinto](/wiki/Shinto "Shinto") and [Buddhist](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") priests discourage it.[[8]](#cite_note-Smyers8-8) Rice food sake and other offerings are given to them for her.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shinshi&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

In a book called *Fusō Sakki*, which was written in the middle of the [Heian period](/wiki/Heian_period "Heian period") in Japan, it is mentioned that a person who killed a white fox (known as shiratoume) near the Ise Shrine was exiled to that area. This suggests that there was a belief in spiritual foxes in ancient Japan.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

The "Chujin Harai-kun," a book written in the 12th century, states that the *kami's* messenger is second only to the eight great *kami* and is subordinate to the 100,000 *kami*. Moreover, "Kitakami Yuki-fu," an essay written in the early 19th century, explains that a ritual called hanasui-iwai is held every year on January 15. During this ritual, every new household is given a Shinshi.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

Due to [Shinbutsu shugo](/wiki/Shinbutsu_shugo "Shinbutsu shugo") some buddhist deities have Shinshi such as [Marici](/wiki/Marici_(Buddhism) "Marici (Buddhism)") whose messenger is the boar.[[12]](#cite_note-Dougill-2017-12)

In the modern day they are considered a notable concept for [environmentalism](/wiki/Environmentalism "Environmentalism").[[13]](#cite_note-13)

## Examples

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shinshi&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Examples")]

| Animal | [Kami](/wiki/Kami "Kami") |
| --- | --- |
| [Cow](/wiki/Cow "Cow") | [Tenjin](/wiki/Tenjin_(kami) "Tenjin (kami)") |
| [Fox](/wiki/Fox "Fox") | [Inari Ōkami](/wiki/Inari_Okami "Inari Okami")[[5]](#cite_note-Digital_Museum-5)[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15) |
| [Pigeon](/wiki/Columbidae "Columbidae") | [Hachiman](/wiki/Hachiman "Hachiman") |
| [Sea Snake](/wiki/Sea_snake "Sea snake") | [Izumo-taisha](/wiki/Izumo-taisha "Izumo-taisha")[[16]](#cite_note-16) |
| [Chicken](/wiki/Chicken "Chicken") | [Amaterasu](/wiki/Amaterasu "Amaterasu")[[1]](#cite_note-D.-2011-1)[[2]](#cite_note-Ise_Shrine-2013-2)[[3]](#cite_note-Olderr-2017-3)[[4]](#cite_note-Goto-2015-4) |
| [Deer](/wiki/Deer "Deer") | [Kasuga-taisha](/wiki/Kasuga-taisha "Kasuga-taisha")[[1]](#cite_note-D.-2011-1)[[17]](#cite_note-BMA-17) |
| [Boar](/wiki/Boar "Boar") | [Marici](/wiki/Marici_(Buddhism) "Marici (Buddhism)")[[12]](#cite_note-Dougill-2017-12) |
| [Wolf](/wiki/Wolf "Wolf") | Mitsumine Shrine[[18]](#cite_note-Ono-2011-18) |
| [Monkey](/wiki/Monkey "Monkey") | [Hie Shrine](/wiki/Hie_Shrine "Hie Shrine"),[[18]](#cite_note-Ono-2011-18) [Juzenji](/wiki/Juzenji "Juzenji")[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-UH-Press-2021-20): 198 |
